items.myth: Kernels magically fix bad features or missing labels.
items.reality: Kernels reshape geometry in feature space; they cannot compensate for leaky features, wrong labels, or train/test distribution shift. Data quality still dominates.
items.example: RBF SVM on unscaled raw counts still mis-ranks neighbors if one column has 1000× larger variance.

items.myth: The kernel trick explicitly constructs the feature map φ(x).
items.reality: The trick avoids computing φ; only pairwise K(x, x′) values are used. φ may be infinite-dimensional (RBF) and never stored.
items.example: sklearn SVC(kernel='rbf') never builds an explicit φ matrix — it fills the Gram matrix via rbf_kernel.

items.myth: RBF kernel is always better than linear because it is more complex.
items.reality: Higher-capacity kernels overfit small or noisy datasets and add compute. Linear kernels win on many high-dimensional sparse problems.
items.example: Linear SVM on MNIST-style binarized pixels is competitive; RBF adds cost with marginal gain.

items.myth: Kernel methods and deep learning are unrelated.
items.reality: Both learn or define geometry so simple decision rules work: kernels fix K analytically; neural nets learn φ from data. Modern pipelines often combine both ideas.
items.example: A CNN embedding plus linear SVM head is conceptually φ learned + linear separator in embedding space.
